Summary: In ER+ metastatic breast cancer resistant to endocrine therapy, ESR1 fusions retain the N‑terminal ER DNA‑binding domain and exclude the ligand‑binding domain by fusing diverse C‑terminal partners. These fusion proteins drive estrogen‑independent ER transcriptional activity and promote proliferation, motility, and metastasis. Breast cancer cells and a PDX with these fusions remained sensitive to CDK4/6 inhibition, suggesting adjuvant CDK4/6i may prevent acquisition. ESR1 ligand‑binding domain mutations such as Y537S drive estrogen‑independent metabolic rewiring with elevated tricarboxylic acid cycle activity, glucose independence, and reliance on glutamine compared with wild‑type ESR1 cells. Antiestrogens induced a metabolically dormant, self‑renewal‑deficient state with suppressed ER expression. Chronic fulvestrant exposure increased paracrine IL‑6 and IL‑6 elevated oxidative phosphorylation to restore growth in quiescent CD133hi/ERlo/Mito lo cells in an ER‑independent manner. PROTACs bind a region shared by mutant and wild‑type ESR1 to recruit an E3‑ubiquitin ligase and induce degradation. ARV‑471 degrades Y537S and D538G and inhibits tumor growth in a patient‑derived xenograft with Y537S, and the authors speculate that earlier use of agents targeting both mutant and wild‑type ER may prevent ligand‑binding domain mutation acquisition.
References: Hanker, A. B., Sudhan, D. R., & Arteaga, C. L. (2020). Overcoming Endocrine Resistance in Breast Cancer. Cancer Cell, 37(4), 496-513. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccell.2020.03.009
